caveats: a: Autonomous haulage evidence spans large global mines and multiple commodities.
a: O*NET covers excavating and loading operators rather than every mine occupation.
a: Automation may shift labor to remote operations, maintenance, and systems support rather than eliminate it.
rho: No representative LMM U.S. surface-coal AI adoption study was found.
rho: Greenfield fleets are easier to automate than mixed legacy equipment.
rho: Regulatory and customer approval can slow operational changes.
e: The strongest fee-based examples are specialized and may not represent small independent mines.
e: Long-term coal supply is recurring but still product revenue.
e: Contract support activity can fall in NAICS 213113 rather than 212114.
s5: The dataset intentionally provides no n-band estimate.
s5: Cross-industry owner intentions are not observed coal-mine transactions.
s5: Asset sales in bankruptcy may not be viable going concerns.
q: Some cost-plus structures pass savings directly to the customer.
q: Dedicated-mine contracts create bilateral dependence rather than supplier power alone.
q: Spot and export pricing sharply reduce capture.
d5: A two-year STEO is not a five-year industry forecast.
d5: National coal trends may understate resilience of low-cost surface basins or metallurgical products.
d5: Weather, gas prices, regulation, exports, and power demand create wide uncertainty.
o: Separating AI effects from broader energy policy and technology trends is highly uncertain.
o: Thermal and metallurgical coal have different substitution pathways.
o: Mine-level obsolescence can be severe even when the commodity remains in use.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include electric generators, industrial facilities, and steel producers. Revenue is typically recognized per ton delivered under spot or term supply contracts; the service-like subset earns inflation-linked management fees or agreed profit per ton under long-duration, dedicated-mine arrangements that may reimburse operating, capital, and reclamation costs.
lens.excluded_activities: Underground coal mines; contract support activities classified in NAICS 213113; pure mineral royalties and brokers; captive mines with no transferable customer relationship; speculative undeveloped reserves; spot-only commodity production; and large diversified or publicly traded coal groups outside the LMM band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market surface coal operators and beneficiators, emphasizing mines serving adjacent power or industrial facilities under long-term cost-reimbursement, management-fee, requirements, or indexed supply agreements, together with specialized high-quality coal programs where reliability, consistent specifications, and mine-to-customer logistics matter.

a.bridge: O*NET surface-mining tasks are translated into total labor-hour exposure, while Caterpillar documents deployed autonomous haulage, fleet assignment, equipment tracking, production recording, material management, maintenance, and safety systems, including use in coal. The evidence demonstrates technical scope but not representative labor savings for U.S. LMM coal mines.
caveats: Autonomous haulage evidence spans large global mines and multiple commodities.
O*NET covers excavating and loading operators rather than every mine occupation.
Automation may shift labor to remote operations, maintenance, and systems support rather than eliminate it.
a.change_evidence: Representative task-time and deployment studies from U.S. surface coal mines measuring operator hours, dispatch, cycle time, tons per machine, fuel, maintenance, downtime, quality, safety, reclamation, and administrative labor attributable to AI or autonomy.
a.rationale: AI and autonomy can address geological modeling, mine planning, blast design, dispatch, truck haulage, loading coordination, stockpile and quality blending, equipment monitoring, predictive maintenance, safety observation, environmental reporting, scheduling, procurement, and documentation. Drilling, blasting, excavation, equipment repair, sampling, preparation, reclamation, weather response, and high-consequence supervision remain physical or expert-controlled.

d5.bridge: EIA's July 2026 outlook forecasts U.S. coal production falling from 528 million short tons in 2025 to 511 million in 2026 and 492 million in 2027, with power-sector consumption also declining. Those near-term national forecasts are extrapolated cautiously to five-year real demand for the LMM surface subset; metallurgical, export, and dedicated-mine outcomes can differ.
caveats: A two-year STEO is not a five-year industry forecast.
National coal trends may understate resilience of low-cost surface basins or metallurgical products.
Weather, gas prices, regulation, exports, and power demand create wide uncertainty.
d5.change_evidence: Independent 2026-2031 U.S. coal production and consumption forecasts by surface versus underground, basin, thermal versus metallurgical, domestic versus export, mine-cost curve, power-plant retirement schedule, and dedicated-mine contracts.
d5.rationale: Coal still supports dispatchable generation, industrial heat, and steelmaking, and data-center electricity growth may slow some retirements. Nevertheless, power-plant retirements, renewable and gas competition, emissions policy, high inventories, efficiency, financing constraints, and customer concentration create a structurally negative U.S. thermal outlook. High-quality metallurgical coal and dedicated cost-advantaged mines provide partial resilience.

e.bridge: NACCO documents dedicated surface lignite mines with fee-based management contracts, inflation-linked fees per ton, customer-funded operating and capital costs, and predictable long-term cash flow; Peabody documents widespread long-term supply agreements with reliability, service, quality, and predictable pricing terms. These large-company structures are mapped to the share of LMM surface operators with recurring service-like economics and may overstate prevalence.
caveats: The strongest fee-based examples are specialized and may not represent small independent mines.
Long-term coal supply is recurring but still product revenue.
Contract support activity can fall in NAICS 213113 rather than 212114.
e.change_evidence: Establishment-level production and revenue by management-fee, cost-plus, requirements, multi-year fixed or indexed supply, spot sale, and export contracts, with mine life, renewal history, and NAICS classification.
e.rationale: A dedicated mine integrated with an adjacent plant, or a supplier embedded through coal quality, rail logistics, and requirements contracts, can have long-duration recurring revenue. Most ownership of coal reserves and sale of commodity tons remains a product and resource-risk business, while contract mining on a fee basis may be classified outside this NAICS, keeping eligibility low.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 21211: labor compensation (L02) $6,304.2M / sectoral output (T30) $26,895.2M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.2936 (margins.json[21]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Metals & Mining EBITDA/Sales 29.36%); revenue/employee $700,401 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 21211); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Separating AI effects from broader energy policy and technology trends is highly uncertain.
Thermal and metallurgical coal have different substitution pathways.
Mine-level obsolescence can be severe even when the commodity remains in use.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that AI specifically accelerates coal-plant retirement, industrial fuel substitution, or steel-process changes beyond baseline energy-transition assumptions, net of AI-related electricity demand.
o.rationale: AI does not directly eliminate the physical extraction and beneficiation of coal and can raise electricity demand, but it may accelerate grid optimization, renewable integration, equipment efficiency, and customer transition away from coal. General coal substitution and plant retirement are primarily captured in demand rather than AI-specific obsolescence, so the incremental obsolescence discount is modest.

q.bridge: NACCO's contracts reimburse costs and pay inflation-linked management fees or agreed profit per ton, while Peabody's agreements address reliability, service, quality, quantity, pricing reopeners, permitted supply sources, and extensions. These mechanisms are mapped to AI-value retention in the eligible subset; large dedicated mines may have stronger contracts than LMM operators.
caveats: Some cost-plus structures pass savings directly to the customer.
Dedicated-mine contracts create bilateral dependence rather than supplier power alone.
Spot and export pricing sharply reduce capture.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level evidence on productivity-sharing, cost reimbursement, management fees, price reopeners, inflation indices, quality penalties, renewals, customer concentration, and margin retention following automation.
q.rationale: Cost-reimbursed fee models can preserve savings only if contract terms let the operator retain productivity gains; ordinary coal supply customers will seek lower delivered cost through bids, reopeners, indices, and fuel substitution. Mine proximity, specific quality, transport constraints, and reliability improve capture, while concentrated utilities, transparent coal prices, and plant-retirement risk weaken it.

caveats: No representative LMM U.S. surface-coal AI adoption study was found.
Greenfield fleets are easier to automate than mixed legacy equipment.
Regulatory and customer approval can slow operational changes.
rho.change_evidence: Mine-level adoption, retrofit cost, validation time, utilization, remote-operator staffing, safety, maintenance, fuel, productivity, and payback evidence separated by fleet size, mine life, terrain, and thermal versus metallurgical output.
rho.rationale: Surface mines offer controlled routes, repetitive haul cycles, large equipment, and measurable production flows that favor autonomy and optimization. LMM realization is constrained by retrofit and communications cost, mixed fleets, variable geology and weather, short reserve lives, union and workforce considerations, safety regulation, cyber risk, integration with blasting and loading, specialist shortages, and weak investment incentives in a declining thermal market.

s5.bridge: Gallup's cross-industry five-year owner-disposition survey is mapped to actionable availability among qualifying surface coal operators. It likely overstates supply because permits, reclamation bonding, reserve quality, customer concentration, equipment condition, environmental liabilities, financing constraints, and secular demand decline make a nominal seller difficult to transact.
caveats: The dataset intentionally provides no n-band estimate.
Cross-industry owner intentions are not observed coal-mine transactions.
Asset sales in bankruptcy may not be viable going concerns.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year U.S. surface-coal transactions, closures, bankruptcies, permit transfers, succession outcomes, and failed sales, including enterprise size, reserve life, reclamation bonding, customer contracts, environmental liabilities, and buyer type.
s5.rationale: No defensible frozen firm count is available. A financeable target must combine transferable mineral and surface rights, permits and bonds, adequate reserves, maintained fleet and preparation assets, a durable customer contract, manageable reclamation and water obligations, and credible community and labor relationships. These requirements imply a thin actionable market.

principal_driver: Autonomous haulage and mine optimization can materially lower cost per ton in repetitive, equipment-intensive surface operations.
principal_weakness: Secular thermal-coal contraction and large, difficult-to-transfer reclamation and customer risks can overwhelm productivity gains.

sources.supports: Industry boundary for surface mining, mine development, and beneficiation of bituminous coal, lignite, and anthracite, with contract support activities excluded.
sources.title: NAICS 212114 - Surface Coal Mining

sources.supports: Direct occupational evidence on surface excavation and loading, equipment inspection and maintenance, coordination, material movement, outdoor and hazardous work, machine control, and physical task content.
sources.title: Excavating and Loading Machine and Dragline Operators, Surface Mining

sources.supports: Deployed mining-autonomy evidence for coal and other commodities covering haulage, fleet assignment, tracking, production records, material management, maintenance, safety, productivity, and fuel use.
sources.title: Cat MineStar Command for Autonomous Hauling

sources.supports: Surface-coal service-model proxy with dedicated mines, inflation-linked management fees per ton, customer-funded operating, capital and reclamation costs, predictable cash flow, and compensated reclamation services.
sources.title: NACCO Industries 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Coal customers, long-term and spot supply structures, backlog, reliability and service rationale, pricing and quality terms, and U.S. thermal market exposure.
sources.title: Peabody Energy 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Cross-industry owner-age and five-year sale, transfer, closure, and succession-intention evidence.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Official observed and forecast U.S. coal production, consumption, inventories, coal-fired generation, wind, and solar capacity through 2027.
sources.title: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook: Coal Markets, July 2026

title: Surface Coal Mining
